coredata.dc:title: In-situ preparation of molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensing test strips for on-site detection of tyramine in vinegar
coredata.dc:creator: Qiao, Dan
Zhang, Zhen
Wang, Lingshuang
Sheng, Wei
Deng, Qiliang
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: Test strips as one of micro-devices are widely concerned in the fields of point-of-care detection and in-field diagnosing. For the purpose of on-site detection of tyramine, a carbon dots-molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensing test strip with high selectivity has been successfully obtained via a rapid in-situ preparation procedure (about 2.5 h). Here, the layer composed of carbon dots-molecularly imprinted polymer (CDs-MIP) with the function of the recognition and signal transduce has been formed on the surface of the filter paper strips via the sol-gel method. The developed test strip displayed a high specificity (Imprinting factor, IF = 4.96); and its fluorescence emission was quenched linearly with the increase of tyramine concentration from 0.5 to 10.0 mg L−1. The limit of detection (LOD) is 0.059 mg L−1 (3σ/s). The application potential was verified by sensing of tyramine in rice vinegar and white rice vinegar with the recoveries of 90.9-104.4% (RSD < 5.8%). Many advantages including a rapid preparation and high selectivity make the fluorescence strip suitable for on-site detection of tyramine. In addition, the preparation procedure can also be easily transferred to the preparation of the fluorescence strip toward other targets.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymer
In-situ preparation
Fluorescence sensor
Test strips
Tyramine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of the CDs-MIP test strips
Selectivity and specificity of the fluorescent test strips
The stability and replicate measurements of the test strips
Analysis of tyramine in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key R and D Program of China (Project No. 2016YFD0401204 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the construction of the CDs-MIP fluorescent test strips.
SEM images of bare filter paper strip (A) and CDs-MIP test strip (B).

Fluorescence response of carbon dots-molecularly imprinted and non-imprinted test strips for tyramine and its structural analogs (A); Chemical structure of Tyramine (a), Dopamine (b), Tyrosine (c) and Histamine (d) (B); Effect of the presence of different concentration tyrosine in adsorption of tyramine on carbon dots-molecularly imprinted and non-imprinted test strips (C). Error bars were obtained from three parallel measurements. Fluorescence stability of carbon dots-molecularly imprinted test strips (D).
Fluorescence emission spectrum of carbon dots-molecularly imprinted test strips (A) and carbon dots-non-imprinted test strips (B) with addition of the different concentration of tyramine.
Fluorescence dependence of carbon dots(A), UV absorption curve of carbon dots and tyramine(B).
Optimization of the amount of solvent in the reaction system.
Optimization of the molar proportion of template, functional monomer and cross-linker.
Optimization of the amount of CDs in the reaction system.
CDs-MIP test strips for the recovery of tyramine in real samples (All results are means of three replicate measurements.).

head.ce:title: In-situ preparation of molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensing test strips for on-site detection of tyramine in vinegar

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Ministry of Education, College of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Ministry of Education, College of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
College of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Test strips as one of micro-devices are widely concerned in the fields of point-of-care detection and in-field diagnosing. For the purpose of on-site detection of tyramine, a carbon dots-molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensing test strip with high selectivity has been successfully obtained via a rapid in-situ preparation procedure (about 2.5 h). Here, the layer composed of carbon dots-molecularly imprinted polymer (CDs-MIP) with the function of the recognition and signal transduce has been formed on the surface of the filter paper strips via the sol-gel method. The developed test strip displayed a high specificity (Imprinting factor, IF = 4.96); and its fluorescence emission was quenched linearly with the increase of tyramine concentration from 0.5 to 10.0 mg L−1. The limit of detection (LOD) is 0.059 mg L−1 (3σ/s). The application potential was verified by sensing of tyramine in rice vinegar and white rice vinegar with the recoveries of 90.9-104.4% (RSD < 5.8%). Many advantages including a rapid preparation and high selectivity make the fluorescence strip suitable for on-site detection of tyramine. In addition, the preparation procedure can also be easily transferred to the preparation of the fluorescence strip toward other targets.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) coupled with the fluorescent substances as sensors are increasingly popular in the fields of chemical and biological detection. MIPs with specific recognition sites can endow sensor with high selectivity, and fluorescent substances as analytic signal reporters provide sensitive response to external stimuli [1]. In addition, paper-based test strips with large specific surface area, short binding time, visualization, easy to storage and transport are arousing more and more interests in the fields of on-site detection [2–6]. Nevertheless, the test strips based on molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensing micro-device have been rarely reported. The initial research of combining MIP-QDs with solid phase carrier (polyimide-based test strip) was reported by Chen [7]. Subsequently, various molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensing test strips such as MIPs/CDs-grafted paper [6], fluorescent chitosan nanoparticles [8], nitrogen-rich quantum dots/ MIPs strip [9], and MIPs test strip with a dual-emission signals [10] have been constructed. However, some drawbacks also limit the development of the fluorescent test strip, for example, MIPs physically adsorbed to the paper strips easily fall off due to the weak interaction [11], and the prepared process is usual time-consuming (more than 3 h).

Tyramine, one low-molecular biogenic monoamine, is produced by the decarboxylation of tyrosine [12,13]. Such compound mainly exists in fermented foods, such as soy sauce, aged cheese, red wine and beer, and can also be formed in meat and fish [13–17]. Moreover, high level of tyramine not only changes the flavor and the composition of food, but also causes diseases, such as hypotension, migraine and cardiac failure [16,18,19]. Some studies have confirmed that tyramine caused toxic effects on human life at a high concentration level (100 to 800 mg kg−1) [20–22]. Various methods such as thin layer chromatography (LC) [23], gas chromatography (GC) [24], high performance liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [25,26], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [27,28] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [29] have been developed for the detection of tyramine. Some other investigations such as MIPs modified carbon electrode, ITO/APTES/ErGO electrode [30,31], electrochemical enzyme biosensors based on calcium phosphate materials [32] and MIP functionalized CdSe/ZnS quantum dots-graphene [33] have also been reported. However, these methods are limited to complicated sample preparation and time-consuming analysis procedure. A rapid, semi-quantitative and effective method is crucial to in situ tyramine detection.

The goal of the research is to develop a CDs-MIP fluorescent sensing test strip for the rapid, highly selective, and sensitive detection of tyramine. The filter paper grafted with double bonds was used as a carrier, the CDs-MIP was formed on the surface of the carrier via in-situ sol-gel method. Eventually, the resulted test strips were successfully applied to determine tyramine in the real samples and showed potential for on-site detection.

Tyramine (98%) was purchased from TCI Development Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). L-tyrosine (99%) and histamine hydrochloride (99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Dopamine hydrochloride (98%) was obtained from J&K Chemical Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Anhydrous citric acid (99.5%) was obtained from Alfa Aesar Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, 98%), γ-methacryloxypropyl trimethoxysilane (γ-MAPS), aqueous ammonia (25%) and ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS, 98%) was purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Whatman double circle qualitative filter papers (medium speed) were obtained from General Electric Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Hangzhou, China). All other chemical reagents were at least analytical grade and used directly. Ultrapure water was obtained from a Millipore-QSPTOC purification system (Millipore, USA). Rice vinegar and white rice vinegar were obtained from a local supermarket and stored at 4 °C.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded by a Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer, the excitation wavelength was set at 360 nm, the slit widths of excitation and emission monochromators were both at 10 nm, and the photomultiplier tube voltage was 700 V (Hitachi, Japan). UV-Vis absorption spectra were measured by Evolution 300 UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA). Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images were observed using a Hitachi SU1510 electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). All optical measurements were performed at room temperature.

The filter paper was treated by cutting into 0.9 × 2.0 cm, and dried in a vacuum oven for 30 min to remove moisture. Then, the dried filter paper was put into ethanol containing γ-MAPS (20%, v/v). After incubated for 1 h, the filter paper was rinsed with ethanol for three times and dried.

The CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method referring to the previous literature [34]. Herein, anhydrous citric acid (carbon source) was dissolved in ultrapure water (26.0 mL) and deaerated with nitrogen for 15 min. Then, APTES (4.0 mL) as the functionalized reagent was injected. The system was stirred for 10 min, and heated at 200 °C for 2 h. The prepared CDs was purified with a dialysis bag (1 kDa) and stored at 4 °C.

CDs-MIP test strips were prepared by in-situ synthesis of CDs-MIP on the surface of pretreated filter paper via sol-gel method. Briefly, tyramine (1.0 mM) was dissolved in ethanol (10.0 mL), and the mixture was sonicated for 10 min. Then, APTES (8.0 mM) and CDs (1.0 mL) acted as the functional monomer and the fluorescence transducer were added under stirring (800 rpm). After 10 min, TEOS (8.0 mM) and aqueous ammonia (100.0 μL, 25%) acted as cross-linker and catalyst were injected into the reaction system and deaerated with nitrogen for 15 min. Eventually, the pretreated filter paper was put into the mixture, and the reaction was carried out under sonication for 30 min at room temperature.

To remove the unreacted excess reagents, CDs-MIP test strips were rinsed with ethanol. Then, the template molecule was extracted by ethanol 80% (v/v) under sonication, until no tyramine was detected in the eluents by UV-Vis spectrophotometer. The test strips were dried under vacuum at 60 °C. As a control, non-imprinted test strips (CDs-NIP) were prepared identically without the addition of the template molecule.

In a typical experiment, the CDs-MIP test strips were immersed in the different concentrations of tyramine solution (5.0 mL). After 30 min, the test strips were rinsed with ethanol and the fluorescence intensity was monitored at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

Rice vinegar and white rice vinegar were selected as the real samples. Prior to the measurement, the samples were diluted 5-folds with ethanol. Then, the samples were spiked with the different concentration levels of tyramine (1.0, 5.0 and 7.5 mg L−1) and treated by vortex for 10 min at 3000 rpm. At last, the CDs-MIP test strips were immersed into the above solutions, and maintained for 30 min. The fluorescence intensities of the test strips were measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the CDs-MIP test strips
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-MIP fluorescent sensing test strips for detection tyramine were developed by in-situ synthesis of CDs-MIP via sol-gel method. Fig. 1 showed the schematic of the synthesis of CDs-MIP test strips. Specially, the filter papers were easily silanized due to the abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of the cellulose fibers [3,35]. In order to form the well-distributed recognition layer, the bare filter paper strips were treated with γ-MAPS (Fig. S1). Besides, methacrylate group was also introduced, which can interact with template via hydrogen and hydrophobic interaction. The residual Si-OH can facilitate the grafting of MIPs layer to the surface of the filter. CDs were used as the fluorescent sensing substance, APTES and TEOS serviced as the functional monomer and cross-linked, respectively. Aqueous ammonia and ethanol acted as the catalyst and the solvent, respectively. The CDs-MIP layer was grafted onto the surface of the pre-treated filter paper (Fig. S2).

From SEM images ( Fig. 2 A), we could observe that the bare filter paper strip exhibits a disorderly distributed three-dimensional network structure. The formed polymers could be clearly observed on the surface of the paper fibers treated with the pre-polymerization mixture (Fig. 2B and Fig. S3). The results confirmed that the CDs-MIP test strips were successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and specificity of the fluorescent test strips
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the selectivity and specificity of the fluorescent test strips, structural analogs of tyramine including dopamine, histamine and tyrosine were employed to conduct the selective experiments. The chemical structures of the above substances were showed in Fig. 3 A. The results demonstrated that the F0/F value of the test strips was the largest for tyramine, also meaning the best selective recognition performance (Fig. 3B). In particular, the CDs-MIP test strips had a relatively higher selectivity to dopamine, one explanation for this could be that the structure of dopamine is more similar with that of tyramine.

Tyramine is generally formed by decarboxylation of tyrosine, and the coexistence of the two compounds is usually encountered for the real samples. Therefore, the specificity of the imprinted and non-imprinted test strips to tyramine was investigated in the mixtures of tyrosine and tyramine. The results indicated that the fluorescence quenching degree of CDs-MIP test strips had a little change with the increase of tyrosine concentration (Fig. 3C). Therefore, the interference caused by tyrosine is neglected and the test strips have an excellent specific recognition performance to tyramine. When the molar ratio is 2, the abnormal fluorescence quenching value is attributed to the interaction between the two compounds (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability and replicate measurements of the test strips
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the test strips is considered as the key property for the application in real samples. To confirm the stability, the test strips were stored at room temperature in the dark, their fluorescence emission intensities were monitored every day for a week. As shown in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence emission intensity of the test strips changed slightly, thus manifested that the test strips have a great stability.

The replicate measurements of the test strips were verified by two manners. For one, the same batch test strips were applied to determine tyramine (5.0 mg L−1), and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is 5.19%. For another, the different batches of test strips were utilized to sense tyramine (5.0 mg L−1), the RSD of 5.61% is obtained. These results indicate that the test strips possess excellent repeatability.

As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, the fluorescence emission intensity of the test strips linearly changed with the change of tyramine concentration. The fluorescence intensity of the imprinted and non-imprinted test strips had a gradually decrease with the concentration increase of tyramine. In comparison to the CDs-MIP test strips, the fluorescence quenching degree of the CDs-NIP test strips were much lower. In addition, tyramine, which contains amine and phenol group, is a typical electron-donating compound. Thus, the emission wavelength of the test strip shifts to short wavelength with the increase of tyramine concentration.

According to Stern-Volmer collision quenching theoretical equation [36],
F 0
/ F =
K
sv
C + l
where, F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of the test strips in the absence of tyramine, and F represents the fluorescence intensity of the test strips in the presence of tyramine; KSV is the fluorescence quenching constant and equals to the imprinting factor (IF), which is used to evaluate the recognition performance of test strips in the present experiment; and C is the concentration of tyramine, ranges from 0.5 to 10.0 mg L−1.

The calibration plot was described in the inset of Fig. 4A, the quenching equation is F0/F = 0.0627C + 0.9769, with a concentration range from 0.5 to 10.0 mg L−1 and the correlation coefficient of 0.9963. The limit of detection (LOD) of the CDs-MIP test strip is 0.059 mg L−1 (3σ/s, σ represents the standard deviation of the blank measurements and s represents the slope of calibration plot). Besides, the fluorescence quenching degree of the non-imprinted test strip was smaller than that of the imprinted one. The reason was attributed to the fact that the non-imprinted test strips was prepared in the absence of the template, which lead to the lack of specific recognition and binding sites corresponding to tyramine.

It is vital for the CDs-MIP test strips to be used to on-site detection. To demonstrate the property, the CDs-MIP test strips prior to and after bonded tyramine (100 mg kg−1, the toxic concentration to people) were irradiated under a 365 nm ultraviolet. Specially, the fluorescence quenching could be observed with the naked-eye after the fluorescence test strip was incubated with tyramine (Fig. 4C). Therefore, the CDs-MIP test strips is potential for the on-site detection.

The prepared CDs display a dependence on the excitation wavelength (Fig. 5 A). Firstly, as the excitation wavelength increases, the fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs increases firstly and then subsequently decreased. Secondly, a red shift of the maximum emission wavelength is significantly observed. Furthermore, when the excitation wavelength is set at 360 nm, the fluorescence emission is the strongest and the emission wavelength is at 465 nm.

To underlie the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the fluorescence test strips caused by tyramine, the UV spectra of CDs, tyramine and the CDs-MIPs were assessed (Fig. 5B and Fig. S5). The results revealed that the ultraviolet absorption peak of tyramine had no overlapped with the emission peak of the CDs (about 465 nm), therefore, the fluorescence quenching caused by an energy transfer was excluded in the present research. In addition, there was an overlap of the UV absorption at about 210 nm between the CDs and tyramine, hence one potential mechanism of the fluorescence quenching was that tyramine acted as an electron donor, the electrons on amino and carboxyl groups were transferred to the electron acceptor (CDs) [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of tyramine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of the CDs-MIP test strips was further verified by determining tyramine in real samples (rice vinegar and white rice vinegar). Recovery tests were carried out by spiking the samples with various amounts of tyramine (1.0, 5.0, 7.5 mg L−1). The results listed in Table 4 presented that the satisfactory recoveries of the spiked samples attained between 90.9% and 104.4% with the RSD of 3.9% to 5.8%, which illustrated the CDs-MIP test strips with a great potential for the sensing of tyramine in real samples.

In this research, a CDs-MIP fluorescence sensing test strip for the detection of tyramine was prepared on the surface of the filter paper via in-situ polymerization. The obtained test strips display many advanced properties for the on-site determining tyramine in real samples, such as a short preparation time (2.5 h), a high imprinting factor (4.96), a relatively low LOD of 0.059 mg L−1 and satisfactory recoveries of 90.9-104.4%. Furthermore, the present approach can easily transfer to others targets monitoring by replacing tyramine with desired targets.

Dan Qiao: Validation, Writing - original draft. Zhen Zhang: Methodology, Data curation. Lingshuang Wang: Validation. Wei Sheng: Validation. Qiliang Deng: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Shuo Wang: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key R and D Program of China (Project No. 2016YFD0401204).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105638.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strategies of molecular imprinting-based fluorescence sensors for chemical and biological analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Yang, J. Li, X. Wang, H. Peng, H. Xiong, L. Chen, Strategies of molecular imprinting-based fluorescence sensors for chemical and biological analysis, Biosens. Bioelectron. 112 (2018) 54-71. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2018.04.028.
